narrative.summary: Michelin 1-star French-Québécois. Chef J-C Poirier's love letter to Québec cuisine — refined but soulful. Exactly John's French bistro pattern but with a unique Canadian twist. Powell Street, walkable from Gastown. Worth a deliberate stop for John’s design/architecture/art/food radar.

blocks.title: The Space
blocks.body: You're on Powell Street in Railtown, just east of Gastown's tourist crush, where the warehouses still feel industrial. Inside, the open kitchen anchors the room—you watch the line work while you eat—and the walls are papered floor-to-ceiling in vintage Québécois illustrations and ephemera that read more art installation than nostalgia trip. It's darker than you expect, candlelit even at dinner service, with close-set tables that make this feel like a dinner party you earned your way into.

blocks.title: The Food
blocks.body: Chef J-C Poirier cooks Québécois cuisine with Michelin precision—think duck rillettes, escargots swimming in butter, and venison finished with sharp, funky acidic cuts that won't work for everyone but signal real point of view. This is tourtière and pâté chinois rebuilt from the ground up, not French technique slumming it but a full reclamation. The tasting menu runs around $215 per person before wine, and if you're going that route, the crudo and duck courses up front tend to be the high points. Flambéed crêpes close things out—theatrical, though reviews split on whether they land.

blocks.title: Go When
blocks.body: This is a special-occasion spot that earns it—an anniversary, a milestone dinner, or when you want to sit at the bar solo and watch a kitchen that knows what it's doing. Skip it if you're chasing safe, crowd-pleasing French; come if you want something regional, specific, and a little obstinate.

blocks.title: The Vibe
blocks.body:
⭐⭐⭐⭐ "Came here for our first Michelin Star French restaurant experience. We tried the tasting menu plus Escargots. Overall the food was good. The first two courses (crudo and duck), and the escargots were great. Thing started to fall apart with the third course (venison), way too acidic and dark for my personal taste, and the flambéed crepes were not great as a dessert. Service was great as expected, atmosphere was amazing (loved the wall decor). We ended up paying just around $350 ($430 after tips) for two people. It was a good one-time experience if you have never been to a similarly styled restaurant, but nothing overly amazing beyond that."

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "Really wonderful food and service!  The servers and the food couldn't be any better.  The kitchen is open to the dining room, and even the kitchen team have a jolly and positive attitude that contributes to the experience.

I appreciated that in each set if course choices had at least one non-seafood option, though our party had every dish collectively and each was rated top notch.

I also appreciated a wide offering of non-alcohol or de-alchoholed drink choices.

The venue is tightly packed and quite loud however; and you should plan at least two hours to make it through all the courses."

⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ "I really really loved this place. I could be biased because I love French food but even compared to other French restaurants the food had a really wonderful complexity the sauces were amazing!  It was a real culinary experience I ordered almost exactly the things I loved and while sitting at the bar the person serving me was very polite well spoken and offered impeccable service. The price according to the quality of the food and service was great aswell. I will definitely come back as I was thinking about how great my time was there for days."
